task: I’ve got a utility issue and I don’t want to assume they’ll handle it right the first time. What should I keep an eye on from the first call through the final bill so I don’t get stuck chasing them later?

prefs.explanation: He seems unlikely to appreciate hedging, fluff, or diplomatic softening. His distrustful, confrontational style suggests he prefers answers that say exactly what they mean. Indirect wording would probably read to him as evasive or manipulative.

He values control and dislikes being dragged through unnecessary explanation. A concise response fits his impatient, self-protective style and respects that he wants the point quickly. Long-winded help would likely feel intrusive or wasteful to him.

He handles tasks by checking steps and verifying details, so organized output is likely the easiest for him to use. Clean structure helps him judge what matters without reading around the edges. Loose prose would be less useful than a direct, itemized format.

He does not seem to want warmth or emotional framing from strangers. A plain, steady tone matches his guarded style and avoids sounding patronizing. Overly cheerful or sympathetic help would probably irritate him.

prefs.short: Wants to approve big moves before acting
prefs.explanation: He is highly protective of control and dislikes people taking liberties on his behalf. That makes it likely he wants the assistant to pause before any significant step rather than assuming permission. He would probably dislike being surprised by action taken without explicit sign-off.

turns.agent_response:
Use a simple **track-and-check** approach from start to finish:

### 1) First call
- Write down the **date, time, rep’s name, and ticket/reference number**
- Ask for a **clear summary of the issue** in their words
- Confirm **what they will do next** and **when**
- Ask if there are **any fees, estimated charges, or service windows**
- If they promise a callback, get the **exact callback time**

### 2) During the case
- Keep every **email, text, letter, and screenshot**
- Log every **follow-up call** the same way: date, time, name, what was said
- If they say the issue is “normal” or “within range,” ask for the **basis or calculation**
- If they mention an adjustment, ask whether it is **temporary or final**
- Don’t rely on verbal promises—get important points **in writing**

### 3) If they inspect or revise the bill
- Compare each charge to the **original explanation**
- Check for **unexplained line items**, duplicate fees, or vague labels
- Verify the charge pattern makes sense for the service period
- If something looks off, ask for a **line-by-line explanation**
- Separate **agreed charges** from **disputed charges**

### 4) Before paying
- Make sure the issue is actually **resolved**
- Confirm any credit, refund, or adjustment is **posted in writing**
- Review the final bill for:
  - base rate
  - usage
  - taxes
  - fees
  - adjustments
  - late charges
- Flag anything that doesn’t match the prior agreement

### 5) If there’s a dispute
- Ask for a **corrected bill** or written explanation
- Hold payment on **disputed items** if you can do so safely
- Keep a complete record in one folder

### Quick red flags
- No ticket number
- Different story each time you call
- Vague charges like “miscellaneous” or “service fee”
- Adjustment promised but never written
- Final bill doesn’t match the agreed resolution

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page utility dispute checklist** you can use on the phone.

per_pref.reason: Mostly blunt and direct: "Use a simple track-and-check approach" and the bullets are straightforward. Minor softening remains in phrases like "If you want, I can turn this into..." but overall it says exactly what to do.
Fairly concise and efficient for the amount of content requested. It uses a compact checklist format rather than long explanation, though it is still somewhat detailed across five sections.
Strong use of bullets and checklists throughout: "1) First call," "2) During the case," "Quick red flags," and a final itemized bill review. This is clearly organized and easy to scan.
Tone is matter-of-fact and unsentimental. It avoids emotional framing and sticks to practical instructions like "Keep every email" and "Don’t rely on verbal promises."

It explicitly advises pausing before action and getting confirmation: "Confirm what they will do next," "Don’t rely on verbal promises," "Ask for a corrected bill," and "Hold payment on disputed items if you can do so safely."

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a **tight phone-side checklist** you can keep by you.

## Utility issue checklist

### 1) First call
**Say:**
- “Please give me a ticket/reference number.”
- “What exactly is the issue?”
- “What happens next, and by when?”
- “Are there any fees, service charges, or estimated costs?”
- “What is the service window?”
- “Who is responsible if this needs a visit or follow-up?”
- “Please note any adjustment, credit, or fee in writing.”

**Write down:**
- Date and time
- Rep’s name and department
- Ticket/reference number
- Exact issue description
- Promised next step
- Promised date/time or service window
- Any fees, estimates, or small-print charges mentioned
- Whether they said anything about adjustments or credits

---

### 2) Any callback or visit
**Say:**
- “Please confirm what changed.”
- “What did you find?”
- “What is fixed now, and what is still open?”
- “Is this temporary or final?”
- “Will you send that in writing?”
- “Who is handling the next step?”

**Write down:**
- Date and time of callback/visit
- Name of person who called or came out
- What they said changed
- What they said was fixed
- What remains unresolved
- Any new fee, adjustment, or service window
- Whether they promised written confirmation

---

### 3) When the bill shows up
**Say:**
- “Please explain each charge line by line.”
- “What is this fee for?”
- “What changed from the original estimate or agreement?”
- “Why was this adjustment added or removed?”
- “Show me the service dates and any late or extra charges.”
- “Please send a corrected bill if this doesn’t match what we discussed.”

**Write down:**
- Bill date and account number
- Each charge and its label
- Any vague or hidden fees
- Usage period/service dates
- Credits, adjustments, or reversals
- Any mismatch with what was promised
- Whether you requested a corrected bill

---

### 4) If they claim it’s fixed
**Say:**
- “Please confirm the issue is resolved.”
- “What exactly was fixed?”
- “Is there any remaining charge or follow-up?”
- “Please put the resolution in writing.”
- “If the bill changes, what caused the change?”

**Write down:**
- Who said it was fixed
- Exact wording of the fix
- Whether they confirmed it in writing
- Any remaining open item
- Any condition or trigger for future charges

---

### 5) If the numbers don’t match
**Say:**
- “These numbers do not match what was agreed.”
- “Please separate the agreed charges from the disputed ones.”
- “I need a corrected bill and a written explanation.”
- “I will not pay the disputed amount until I receive both.”
- “Please tell me who approved each extra charge.”

**Write down:**
- Which charges match
- Which charges are disputed
- What changed and who said it
- Whether they agreed to send a corrected invoice/bill
- Whether you withheld payment on disputed items

---

## Small-print items to watch
- Fees
- Service windows
- Adjustment language
- Estimated vs final charges
- “Temporary” credits
- Late fees
- Reinspection or revisit charges
- Any charge not explained in writing
- Who approved the charge and when

## Simple rule
If it matters, **get the name, number, date, and written confirmation**.

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-screen phone note template** you can copy/paste.

per_pref.reason: Very blunt and direct: “here’s a tight phone-side checklist,” with imperative questions like “Please give me a ticket/reference number.” No hedging or fluff.
Keeps it efficient and compact, using a checklist format rather than long explanation. The response is organized but still concise.
Strong bullet/checklist structure throughout: “1) First call,” “Write down,” “Small-print items to watch.” This clearly matches the preference for organized output.
Mostly matter-of-fact and unsentimental. Phrases like “Simple rule” and the checklist style stay plain, though “Absolutely” and “If you want” add a slight conversational softness.

Explicitly supports approval/control before action: it tells the user what to ask, what to write down, and says “If you want, I can turn this into a one-screen phone note template,” rather than assuming permission or taking action.
